query: origin of curare and relation to Strychnos genus

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Indications -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Native peoples in South America historically coated arrows and darts used for hunting with a substance called "curare." Spanish explorers, or Conquistadors, returned home from expeditions to South America, telling tales of a "flying death." In 1562, a writer in the court of King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain was the first to document anecdotal evidence of these poisoned projectiles in a book titled De Orbe Novo . [3] Sir Walter Raleigh, a British explorer, described the use of poisoned arrows in modern-day Venezuela in his 1848 book titled Discovery of the Large, Rich, and Beautiful Empire of Guiana . The word "ourari" was used by one of Sir Walter's lieutenants. [4] [5] Edward Bancroft, another pioneer, brought samples of crude curare from South America back to the Old World.

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Both hydrastine and strychnine inhibit the central nervous system glycine receptor and can also mimic acute tetanus toxicity. In contrast to tetanus toxicity, however, a poisoned individual may exhibit periods of muscle weakness or flaccidity. Sources of strychnine include rodenticides, contaminated heroin or cocaine, and an herbal remedy for arthritis called “maqianzi.” [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Plant chemistry and toxicology are complex with a rich history. Much about plant chemistry remains unknown, and our understanding relies on animal research combined with documented human experiences. The dynamic science of pharmacognosy, which highlights the therapeutic value of plants, has elucidated several major classes of organic molecules found in plants, of which alkaloids represent one. Others include phenols and phenylpropanoids; terpenes and resins; glycosides; and proteins, peptides, and lectins. Plant alkaloids represent a diverse array of secondary plant metabolites exemplified by familiar compounds such as nicotine, caffeine, cocaine, mescaline, ephedrine, and strychnine. Chemically classified as amines, plant alkaloids are defined by their function as bases generally containing one or more nitrogen atoms within a heterocyclic structure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cone Snail Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Conus genus, within the Conidae family, is a group of predatory gastropod mollusks. The spiral shells of the snail are the life-long habitat for the indwelling predator. As the snail continues to grow, it builds upon its patterned shell. All members of the genus appear similar, but many different Conus species fall under this umbrella, some of which pose a greater threat to humans than others. Cone snails range in size from a few centimeters up to 29 cm long. These snails sense prey within their environment using an appendage called a siphon. While some species do have eyestalks, the siphon provides a more sensitive method of locating prey, as well as performing additional respiratory functions.  There is variability in the prey each Conus species tends to hunt. Some feed on worms (vermivores), others on mollusks (molluscivores), and those most toxic to humans feed on fish (piscivores). Knowing these specific feeding patterns makes differentiation of deadly Conus species easier; though all cone snails are capable of envenomation. The geographic cone is the most toxic of the known species, and several human deaths have resulted from envenomation. Humans are not typical cone snail prey and envenomation is most likely to occur during handling. Unsurprisingly, envenomation occurs most often on the palms and fingers. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Postmortem Changes -- Issues of Concern -- Classification of Post Mortem Changes
retrieved_docs.content: Rigor mortis appears rapidly in children and the old aged individuals, in cases of persons dying of diseases or conditions involving great exhaustion such as cholera, or due to convulsions as in cases of strychnine poisoning. In such cases, the rigor disappears early as well. [23] The effect of rigor on individual muscles can be of additional significance. The rigor of erector pilae muscles may cause elevation of hair leading to the pimpled appearance of the skin. This phenomenon is termed ‘cutis anserina’ or ‘goose skin.’ In the heart, rigor causes contraction of ventricles, which may be erroneously interpreted as ventricular hypertrophy. The rigor of the dartos muscle in the scrotum may lead to post mortem ejaculation of semen and may get wrongly interpreted to sexual activity just before death.

retrieved_docs.title: Procidentia(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The first recorded documentation of pelvic organ prolapse dates back to 1550 BC in the Egyptian medical papyrus of herbal knowledge, Ebers Papyrus. [1] Historical management of prolapse varied, including acts of manual manipulating of the prolapsed organ, cleansing the prolapsed organ with oils and wines, and inhaling malodorous fumes. [1] Hippocrates also described a process termed succession, which was a maneuver that placed women upside down on a ladder while the ladder frame moved up and down with the hopes that gravity would restore the pelvic organs to their anatomical position. [1] [2] [3] There have even been multiple reports of primitive vaginal hysterectomies. The most commonly quoted was performed by Soranus of Rome during the 1st century when he completed a vaginal hysterectomy on a gangrenous uterus. [1] [2] However, credit for the first vaginal hysterectomy goes to Capri during the beginning of the 16th century for performing the first partial vaginal hysterectomy for pelvic organ prolapse. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Strychnine Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History should focus on ingestions within the last few hours. History of ingestion of herbal remedies should raise suspicion for strychnine poisoning. Recent cocaine or heroin use can be a useful historical clue, as sometimes illicit drugs are cut with strychnine. Time from exposure should be ascertained to evaluate the usefulness of gastrointestinal decontamination.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents -- Indications -- History
retrieved_docs.content: After studying curare, neurologist Walter Freeman hypothesized that administering curare to induce temporary paralysis could provide relief of muscle spasms and rigidity in patients with multiple sclerosis (MS.) Freeman shared his hypothesis with Richard Gill, a patient with MS, who brought 25 lbs (11 kg) of raw curare from Ecuador. The raw curare was delivered to Squibb & Sons, who attempted to derive an effective antidote to curare. In 1942, Squibb & Sons scientists Wintersteiner and Dutcher isolated the alkaloid d-tubocurarine. Soon after, AH Holladay (also working for Squibb & Sons) developed a standardized commercial preparation of curare named Intocostrin. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Isoquinoline alkaloids include structurally similar sanguinarine, hydrastine, and berberine. Berberine has demonstrated cardiac and respiratory depression, smooth muscle contraction, and uterine contraction. A mass sanguinarine poisoning from the consumption of a Mexican prickly poppy ( Argemone mexicana ) resulted in gastrointestinal symptoms, hepatomegaly, anemia, skin darkening and lesions, erythema, and peripheral edema. On the severe end, affected individuals may experience ascites, congestive heart failure, and myocarditis. Hydrastine ingestion has been reported to cause movement disorders that resemble poisoning by a non-isoquinoline alkaloid, strychnine, found in Strychnos nux-vomica. Poisoning from these alkaloids is associated with muscle spasms, weakness, rigidity, and death via respiratory muscle involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Plant Alkaloids Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Commonly, patients will experience amnesia to events following ingestion. Important to note is that not all characteristics of this toxidrome will necessarily be present in every case. These plant metabolites have been exploited for their hallucinogenic properties recreationally via the consumption of brewed teas and seeds. Plants containing these phytochemicals from the family Solanaceae may also be smoked. Datura stramonium (jimsonweed), the chief source of such seeds, is found in the United States and Southern Canada, and poisoning may lead to acute anticholinergic poisoning and death in both children and adults. Recreational ingestions exceed unintentional ingestions as the main cause of toxicity. The duration of the effect of belladonna poisoning may last from a few hours to days, depending on the dose of exposure. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Cilia Incarnata -- Introduction -- Etymology: Latin Derivatives
retrieved_docs.content: Cilium is a neuter noun whose plural is cilia or cilii). Incarnata is the neuter plural of incarnatus, which is the perfect passive participle of incarno, which means "to make or become flesh." Incarnatum is the first supine of incarno. Internum is the adjective neuter form meaning "internal" or "inward." Externum is the adjective neuter form meaning "external" or "outward."

retrieved_docs.title: Rodenticide Toxicity -- History and Physical -- Strychnine
retrieved_docs.content: Exposure to strychnine may cause muscle spasms, trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonos, lactic acidosis, and hyperthermia. Rhabdomyolysis may also develop due to muscle damage caused by strychnine exposure. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Rattlesnake Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Rattlesnakes are found throughout the Americas. They exist in almost every state of the U.S., except Alaska and Hawaii, and include snakes in the genera Crotalus and Sistrurus within the subfamily Crotalinae, commonly known as pit vipers. Other venomous snake genera within this subfamily include Lanceheads ( Bothrops) , Copperheads/Cottonmouths ( Agkistrodon ), and Asian Palm Pit Vipers ( Trimeresurus ) . Their primary defense mechanism is to hide, but will also rattle and hiss to scare away predators. If they are further challenged, they can bite and envenomate with potentially deadly effects. Knowing the common snakes in one’s area is important in managing a snake bite appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: Mushroom Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term mushroom refers to the fungal fruiting body. Anatomically, the prototypical mushroom comprises the stem and a cap with gills on the underside. However, the term may refer to many stemless gilled fungi with varying forms such as the "morel," "puffball," or "stinkhorn." The gills of the mushroom produce spores which aid in the propagation of the fungus itself.

retrieved_docs.title: Entomophthoromycosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Entomophthoromycosis is a group of infections caused by members of the order Entomophthorales, which comprises 6 families— Ancylistaceae, Basidiobolaceae, Completoriaceae, Entomopthoraceae, Meristacraceae, and Neozygitaceae. Within the Basidiobolaceae family, there is 1 genus with 4 species, and the most common species is B ranarum, commonly found on the skin or dung of frogs. Various species of Conididobolus from the family Ancylistaceae are saprobes found in soil. Notably, C coronatus and B ranarum are the primary species associated with medical cases of entomophthoromycosis. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Cryptococcus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cryptococcus was first identified in peach juice in Italy by Sanfelice in 1894. In 1895, the first human case was reported. This case was a young woman with a chronic, non-healing skin ulcer on her shin. Yeasts were found in her ulcer, and later at autopsy, yeasts were found in multiple internal organs. These yeasts were Cryptococcus neoformans . The sexual form of this fungus was identified in 1976 and named Filobasidiella neoformans . Thus far, 19 species of Cryptococcus have been identified. Two are pathogenic. There are two serotypes in each of these species. Cryptococcus neoformans var. neoformans is serotype D, Cryptococcus neoformans var. grubii is serotype A, and Cryptococcus gattii has two serotypes: B and C. The Cryptococcus neoformans genome was published in 2005. [5] [6] [7] Cryptococcus species are found in soil contaminated by guano. Cryptococcus gattii has a clear association with eucalyptus trees and a variety of coniferous trees.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is classified as a plant agent product, indicating that it originates from or is derived from botanical sources. Buprenorfin is identified both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant agent product, showing that it belongs to the class of bioactive compounds produced by plants and also fits within the broader category of plant‑derived agents. Together, these relationships illustrate that plant agent products encompass a range of substances, including synthetic‑type agents such as karbenoxolon and natural alkaloids like buprenorfin, highlighting the importance of plant‑based classifications in pharmacology and drug development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 817
retrieved_docs.content: Rauwolfia alkaloids represent a class of indole‑derived compounds that include several pharmacologically active constituents such as yohimbin and reserpin. Both yohimbin and reserpin are described as “children” of the Rauwolfia alkaloid family, indicating that they are individual alkaloids derived from plants of the Rauwolfia genus. The broader chemical grouping of Rauwolfia alkaloids is linked to sekologanin‑tryptaminové alkaloidy, suggesting that these two families share structural or biosynthetic features. Several pharmaceutical preparations incorporate Rauwolfia alkaloids as active ingredients. The oral spray formulation containing a complex mixture of botanical extracts—including chaste tree fruit, avena sativa flowering top, turnera diffusa leafy twig, gelsemium sempervirens root, strychnos ignatii seed, lycopodium clavatum spore, bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine (yohimbin)—lists yohimbin as the active

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 519
retrieved_docs.content: as the active ingredient in a complex oral spray formulation that also contains chaste tree fruit, Avena sativa flowering top, Turnera diffusa leafy twig, Gelsemium sempervirens root, Strychnos ignatii seed, Lycopodium clavatum spore, Bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris whole, and other excipients. This multi‑herb spray highlights the therapeutic use of yohimbine alongside numerous botanical extracts. Reserpine is another child alkaloid within the Rauwolfia family, and its relationship to yohimbine is noted, underscoring the close chemical and pharmacologic linkage between these two compounds. The Rauwolfia alkaloids are further related to the broader chemical group termed sekologanin‑tryptamine alkaloids, situating them within a larger family of structurally related natural products. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how Rauwolfia alkaloids serve as core active agents in several distinct pharmaceutical preparations—ranging

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant agent product and is classified within the terpene family. Buprenorfin is recognized as a naturally occurring alkaloid and is also categorized as a plant agent product. These classifications highlight how both karbenoxolon and buprenorfin are derived from plant sources and belong to distinct chemical families—terpenes for karbenoxolon and alkaloids for buprenorfin—underscoring the diversity of bioactive plant compounds relevant to pharmacology and therapeutic research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: as a member distinct from steroids, which occupy a separate structural class. Finally, the natural‑product category Rostlinné pryskyřice (plant resin) comprises derived substances including terpentýn (turpentine), which is a child subcategory of this resinous material.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid, indicating that it is a bioactive chemical compound derived from natural sources with the typical structural features of alkaloids. It is also identified as a plant agent product, emphasizing its origin from botanical material and its role as a plant‑derived substance. Additionally, buprenorfin is described as a phytonutrient, suggesting that it possesses nutritional or therapeutic properties associated with plant‑based nutrients. Recognizing buprenorfin’s overlapping identities as an alkaloid, a plant‑derived product, and a phytonutrient provides a comprehensive understanding of its potential pharmacological effects and its relevance in both medicinal and nutritional contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: Taxanes, antineoplastic are classified as plant alkaloids and other natural‑product–derived antineoplastic drugs, reflecting their origin from natural sources and their role in cancer treatment. Within this taxane class, docetaxel is identified as a member, representing one of the specific agents that exemplify the therapeutic application of taxane antineoplastic compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 645
retrieved_docs.content: Rauwolfia alkaloids constitute a class of naturally occurring alkaloids and plant‑derived agent products. Within this class, specific constituents such as yohimbine (also spelled yohimbin) and reserpine are identified as child compounds. Yohimbine is present as the active ingredient in a complex oral spray formulation that contains extracts of chaste tree fruit, Avena sativa flowering top, Turnera diffusa leafy twig, Gelsemium sempervirens root, Strychnos ignatii seed, Lycopodium clavatum spore, Bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine itself, illustrating its role in multi‑herb preparations. Yohimbine, as a Rauwolfia alkaloid, is also the active component of the combination medication “bendroflumethiazide + rauwolfia + potassium chloride,” where Rauwolfia alkaloids provide the therapeutic effect. Reserpine is likewise a child of the Rauwolfia alkaloid family and may be linked to yohimbine in some classifications, reflecting overlapping membership within the same alkaloid lineage. The broader chemical family that encompasses Rauwolfia alkaloids includes sekologanin‑tryptaminové alkaloidy, indicating a wider structural relationship. Additionally, the pharmaceutical product Abicol tablet is formulated to contain Rauwolfia alkaloids, further illustrating the therapeutic use of this alkaloid class. Buprenorphine (spelled buprenorfin) is classified both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, highlighting that not all therapeutic alkaloids belong to the Rauwolfia group but share similar botanical origins. Together, these relationships delineate the hierarchical taxonomy of alkaloid classes, their incorporation into multi‑ingredient botanical preparations, and their application in combination drugs for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 372
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant‑derived agent that belongs to the terpeny class of compounds, indicating that it is a type of terpenoid produced by plants. Buprenorfin (buprenorphine) is described both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, linking it to the same botanical origin as karbenoxolon. In clinical practice, buprenorfin is employed for the maintenance treatment of opioid dependence, providing long‑term therapy for patients who use opioid substances. The shared classification of these substances as plant‑based agents underscores the relevance of botanical sources in the development of pharmacologically active compounds used for both therapeutic and research purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1806
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant agent product, indicating that it is a therapeutic compound derived from botanical sources and classified within the category of plant‑derived medical agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 744
retrieved_docs.content: Karbenoxolon is identified as a plant‑derived agent product and is classified within the terpene family, indicating that it is a botanical terpene compound used for therapeutic purposes. Ingenol mebutate is also a member of the terpene class, sharing the same chemical family as karbenoxolon. Together, these relationships emphasize that both karbenoxolon and ingenol mebutate are terpene‑based substances, with karbenoxolon specifically recognized as a plant agent product, highlighting their common botanical origin and potential relevance in pharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2161
retrieved_docs.content: The oral spray formulation OHM‑DAM contains a complex blend of herbal and mineral components—including CHASTE TREE FRUIT, AVENA SATIVA FLOWERING TOP, TURNERA DIFFUSA LEAFY TWIG, GELSEMIUM SEMPERVIRENS ROOT, STRYCHNOS IGNATII SEED, LYCOPODIUM CLAVATUM SPORE, BOS TAURUS TESTICLE, PHOSPHORIC ACID, SAW PALMETTO, SELENIUM, TRIBULUS TERRESTRIS WHOLE, and YOHIMBINE—each measured in specific concentrations per 30 mL. This multi‑ingredient mixture is identified as an active ingredient of yohimbin, indicating that the combined preparation contributes to the pharmacological effects attributed to yohimbin. Additionally, yohimbin is described as a “child” of reserpin (reserpine), suggesting that yohimbin is derived from, metabolically related to, or structurally descended from reserpine. Together, these relationships highlight that the OHM‑DAM spray delivers a yohimbin‑based active component whose origins trace back to the alkaloid reserpine, providing insight into its potential mechanism of action and therapeutic context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: A [Preparations] is a group of medicinal preparations that is linked to the alkaloid ajmalicine and the preparation alseroxylon as its parent sources, indicating that both ajmalicine and alseroxylon serve as foundational compounds or origins for this category. In turn, A [Preparations] functions as the parent of the preparation ankurd, making ankurd a derivative or sub‑preparation within the same lineage. This hierarchical relationship illustrates how ajmalicine and alseroxylon contribute to the development of A [Preparations], which subsequently gives rise to ankurd, thereby clarifying the pharmacological ancestry and potential therapeutic connections among these related botanical or alkaloid‑based medicines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Plant agent product is a classification that includes both karbenoxolon and buprenorfin. Karbenoxolon is identified as a member of the terpeny family, indicating that it is a terpenoid compound derived from plant sources. Buprenorfin, while also a plant agent product, is further characterized as a naturally occurring alkaloid, reflecting its origin from plant alkaloid biosynthesis. In addition to these relationships, ingenol mebutate is recognized as a terpeny, placing it within the same chemical class as karbenoxolon, and it is listed as a member of the “Other chemotherapeutics in ATC” group, indicating its inclusion among therapeutic agents classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical system. Together, these connections illustrate how karbenoxolon and ingenol mebutate share terpenoid chemistry, while buprenorfin bridges the categories of plant‑derived agents and natural alkaloids, and all three compounds are relevant to pharmacological classification and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 733
retrieved_docs.content: The chaste tree preparation is classified as a member of the “other gynecologicals in ATC” group, which is a subclass of the broader “GENITO‑URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES” category; this category in turn belongs to the “GYNECOLOGICAL ANTI‑INFECTIVES AND ANTISEPTICS” class. As a gynecological product, the preparation contains a complex mixture of active ingredients and active moieties. In one formulation, the sublingual solution/drops (R18 OVAGEN) lists adenosine‑triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, apis mellifera, chaste tree, ammonium carbonate and lycopodium clavatum spore among its active ingredients. A second oral pellet formulation (Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit) includes an extensive array of substances such as artemisia abrotanum, aconitum napellus, chaste tree, various mineral salts (e.g., ammonium carbonate, antimony trisulfide, barium carbonate), metals (gold, copper, cadmium, cobalt, lead, mercury compounds), plant extracts (e.g., arnica montana, milk thistle, hyoscyamus niger, veratrum album), animal venoms (e.g., Bothrops atrox, Naja naja, Vipera berus), and numerous acids and organic compounds (e.g., hydrofluoric acid, nitric acid, phenol, quinine sulfate). A third formulation described as an oral spray (OHM‑DAM) identifies active moieties such as chaste tree fruit, avena sativa flowering top, turnera diffusa leafy twig, gelsemium sempervirens root, strychnos ignatii seed, lycopodium clavatum spore, bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine. Together, these details illustrate that the chaste tree preparation is a multi‑component gynecological agent, positioned within the ATC hierarchy of sex‑hormone and genitourinary therapies, and formulated with a wide spectrum of botanical, mineral, metallic, and synthetic constituents intended for therapeutic use in women’s health.
